agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A commercial commitment to purchase goods, progressing through a lifecycle from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, preventing it from being fulfilled and shipped."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document that locks in pricing and creates a billing obligation. Finance considers invoicing irreversible."},{"term":"Placement","definition":"The moment an order is created and accepted into the system."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The act of dispatching goods to the customer, marking a fulfillment milestone."},{"term":"Invoiced Order","definition":"An order for which an invoice has been issued. Per Finance, this state is terminal with respect to cancellation."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order may be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from the PM defining the cancellation window. A VP could adjust this policy (e.g., restrict cancellation to within 1 hour of placement).","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, when a cancellation request is submitted, then the order transitions to Cancelled."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An order that has been invoiced cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats invoicing as an irreversible financial commitment. Reversing it would corrupt financial records and violate accounting integrity.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has been invoiced, when a cancellation request is submitted, then the request is rejected and the order remains in its current state."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when to trigger invoicing. The timing could be changed (e.g., invoice at shipment) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a new order is placed, when the placement process completes, then an invoice is immediately generated for that order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been placed and has not yet shipped","when":"A user requests cancellation of the order","then":"The order is cancelled successfully (per PM requirement — BUT this contradicts BR-2 + BR-3)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order has been invoiced","when":"A user requests cancellation of the order","then":"The cancellation is rejected because invoicing is irreversible (per Finance requirement)"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A new order is being placed","when":"The placement process completes","then":"An invoice is automatically generated and associated with the order (per Operations requirement)"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An order has already shipped","when":"A user requests cancellation","then":"The cancellation is rejected because the order has already shipped"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled (race condition with invoicing)","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — if invoicing is synchronous at placement, the order is invoiced before any cancellation can occur, making cancellation impossible per BR-2. If async, a narrow window may exist."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — PM says 'before it ships' but does not address partial shipment scenarios."},{"scenario":"Credit note or reversal after invoicing","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — Finance says invoicing is irreversible, but standard accounting practice allows credit notes. The spec does not address whether a credit-note-based cancellation flow is acceptable."},{"scenario":"Every order placed is immediately uncancellable","expected_behavior":"The logical consequence of BR-2 + BR-3 combined: no order can ever be cancelled, rendering the entire cancellation feature dead on arrival."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If the contradiction is resolved by removing cancellation capability, customers cannot cancel any orders, likely increasing chargebacks, refund disputes, and customer support costs. If resolved by decoupling invoicing from placement, pricing lock-in guarantees may be weakened.","operational":"The cancellation feature cannot be built as specified. Engineering will be blocked until stakeholders align. Any implementation will necessarily violate at least one stakeholder's requirement.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. If the system silently rejects all cancellations due to immediate invoicing, user trust and satisfaction will erode significantly.","risk":"HIGH — shipping any interpretation without stakeholder alignment risks violating financial controls (if we allow cancelling invoiced orders) or violating customer expectations (if we block all cancellations). Regulatory risk if invoicing/cancellation handling conflicts with tax or consumer protection laws.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders despite reasonable expectation they should be able to before shipment. Leads to chargebacks, complaints, and churn."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If cancellation of invoiced orders is permitted, their accounting integrity and revenue recognition processes are disrupted."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoicing is decoupled from placement, their pricing lock-in strategy must be redesigned."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Core feature requirement is undeliverable as specified; must negotiate a revised policy."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Blocked from implementation until the contradiction is resolved. Any guess risks costly rework."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements form a logical impossibility: orders are invoiced at placement (Ops), invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (Finance), but orders should be cancellable before shipment (PM). The transitive closure of BR-2 and BR-3 makes BR-1 impossible. This is not an edge case — it affects 100% of orders. Confidence is low because no correct implementation can satisfy all three requirements simultaneously.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and affects every order, not just edge cases","Each individual requirement is clearly stated and unambiguous in isolation","The domain language (order, invoice, cancellation, shipment) is well-understood"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept a credit-note workflow as a workaround","Whether Operations would accept delaying invoicing to post-cancellation-window","Whether the PM requirement was written with awareness of the invoicing policy"],"assumptions":["Invoicing at placement is synchronous — the order is invoiced before any cancellation window opens","There is no existing credit-note or invoice-reversal mechanism that Finance considers distinct from cancellation","All three requirements are current and authoritative for their respective domains"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) is logically impossible when combined with BR-2 (invoiced orders cannot be cancelled) and BR-3 (orders are invoiced at placement). Since every order is invoiced the instant it is placed, every order is immediately uncancellable, making the cancellation feature a no-op. These are three explicit stakeholder requirements that cannot all be satisfied.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Engineering"],"options":["(A) Finance relaxes BR-2: allow cancellation of invoiced orders via credit notes, treating it as a reversal rather than a cancellation of the invoice itself.","(B) Operations changes BR-3: delay invoicing until after the cancellation window closes (e.g., invoice at shipment or at a cutoff time), preserving cancellability.","(C) PM narrows BR-1: accept that orders cannot be cancelled once placed, and reframe the feature as a return/refund process post-shipment.","(D) Introduce a new state: 'pro-forma invoice' at placement (locks pricing but is reversible), with final invoice generated at shipment (irreversible)."],"recommendation":"Option D — introduce a pro-forma or provisional invoice at placement to lock pricing (satisfying Ops), which is reversible (satisfying PM's cancellation requirement), with the final irreversible invoice generated at a later stage such as shipment (satisfying Finance's invariant). This requires all three stakeholders to agree on the two-stage invoicing model."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated form a logical contradiction that makes the cancellation feature impossible to implement correctly.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is negated by the conjunction of BR-2 and BR-3. No order can ever be in a state that is both pre-shipment and un-invoiced."}]}

Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A commercial commitment to purchase goods, progressing through a lifecycle from placement to shipmen

content.thinking:
Let me analyze these three requirements:

1. PM: "An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships."
2. Finance: "Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled — invoicing is irreversible."
3. Operations: "We invoice orders at the time of placement to lock in pricing."

The contradiction is clear:
- Operations invoices at placement time (so every placed order is immediately invoiced)
- Finance says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled
- Therefore: no order can ever be cancelled (since it's invoiced the moment it's placed)
- But PM says orders can be cancelled before shipping


- This is a direct contradiction between explicit stakeholder positions that needs to be resolved.

The timing could be changed (e.g., invoice at shipment) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a new order is placed, when the placement process completes, then an invoice is immediately generated for that order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been placed and has not yet shipped","when":"A user requests cancellation of the order","then":"The order is cancelled successfully (per PM requirement — BUT this contradicts BR-2 + BR-3)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order has been invoiced","when":"A user requests cancellation of the order","then":"The cancellation is rejected because invoicing is irreversible (per Finance requirement)"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A new order is being placed","when":"The placement process completes","then":"An invoice is automatically generated and associated with the order (per Operations requirement)"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An order has already shipped","when":"A user requests cancellation","then":"The cancellation is rejected because the order has already shipped"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled (race condition with invoicing)","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — if invoicing is synchronous at placement, the order is invoiced before any cancellation can occur, making cancellation impossible per BR-2. If async, a narrow window may exist."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — PM says 'before it ships' but does not address partial shipment scenarios."},{"scenario":"Credit note or reversal after invoicing","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — Finance says invoicing is irreversible, but standard accounting practice allows credit notes. The spec does not address whether a credit-note-based cancellation flow is acceptable."},{"scenario":"Every order placed is immediately uncancellable","expected_behavior":"The logical consequence of BR-2 + BR-3 combined: no order can ever be cancelled, rendering the entire cancellation feature dead on arrival."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If the contradiction is resolved by removing cancellation capability, customers cannot cancel any orders, likely increasing chargebacks, refund disputes, and customer support costs. If resolved by decoupling invoicing from placement, pricing lock-in guarantees may be weakened.","operational":"The cancellation feature cannot be built as specified. Engineering will be blocked until stakeholders align. Any implementation will necessarily violate at least one stakeholder's requirement.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. If the system silently rejects all cancellations due to immediate invoicing, user trust and satisfaction will erode significantly.","risk":"HIGH — shipping any interpretation without stakeholder alignment risks violating financial controls (if we allow cancelling invoiced orders) or violating customer expectations (if we block all cancellations). Regulatory risk if invoicing/cancellation handling conflicts with tax or consumer protection laws.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders despite reasonable expectation they should be able to before shipment. Leads to chargebacks, complaints, and churn."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If cancellation of invoiced orders is permitted, their accounting integrity and revenue recognition processes are disrupted."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoicing is decoupled from placement, their pricing lock-in strategy must be redesigned."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Core feature requirement is undeliverable as specified; must negotiate a revised policy."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Blocked from implementation until the contradiction is resolved. Any guess risks costly rework."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements form a logical impossibility: orders are invoiced at placement (Ops), invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (Finance), but orders should be cancellable before shipment (PM). The transitive closure of BR-2 and BR-3 makes BR-1 impossible. This is not an edge case — it affects 100% of orders. Confidence is low because no correct implementation can satisfy all three requirements simultaneously.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and affects every order, not just edge cases","Each individual requirement is clearly stated and unambiguous in isolation","The domain language (order, invoice, cancellation, shipment) is well-understood"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept a credit-note workflow as a workaround","Whether Operations would accept delaying invoicing to post-cancellation-window","Whether the PM requirement was written with awareness of the invoicing policy"],"assumptions":["Invoicing at placement is synchronous — the order is invoiced before any cancellation window opens","There is no existing credit-note or invoice-reversal mechanism that Finance considers distinct from cancellation","All three requirements are current and authoritative for their respective domains"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) is logically impossible when combined with BR-2 (invoiced orders cannot be cancelled) and BR-3 (orders are invoiced at placement). Since every order is invoiced the instant it is placed, every order is immediately uncancellable, making the cancellation feature a no-op. These are three explicit stakeholder requirements that cannot all be satisfied.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Engineering"],"options":["(A) Finance relaxes BR-2: allow cancellation of invoiced orders via credit notes, treating it as a reversal rather than a cancellation of the invoice itself.","(B) Operations changes BR-3: delay invoicing until after the cancellation window closes (e.g., invoice at shipment or at a cutoff time), preserving cancellability.","(C) PM narrows BR-1: accept that orders cannot be cancelled once placed, and reframe the feature as a return/refund process post-shipment.","(D) Introduce a new state: 'pro-forma invoice' at placement (locks pricing but is reversible), with final invoice generated at shipment (irreversible)."],"recommendation":"Option D — introduce a pro-forma or provisional invoice at placement to lock pricing (satisfying Ops), which is reversible (satisfying PM's cancellation requirement), with the final irreversible invoice generated at a later stage such as shipment (satisfying Finance's invariant). This requires all three stakeholders to agree on the two-stage invoicing model."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated form a logical contradiction that makes the cancellation feature impossible to implement correctly.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is negated by the conjunction of BR-2 and BR-3. No order can ever be in a state that is both pre-shipment and un-invoiced."}]}
